# Brief: Kira (PERSONA-015)
## Upcoming Meeting: 2026-03-30

# Forward-Looking Brief: Kira (PERSONA-015 — Cross-Building Staff Advocate)
**Prepared for meeting: 2026-03-30**

---

## Since Last Meeting

No new inter-meeting evidence has emerged in this period.

That silence is worth noting. The two most consequential data points Kira is carrying — that MTSS roles appear on the first-wave SPTA reduction list, and that the specific status of itinerant/traveling specialist positions remains unresolved — have not been publicly addressed or clarified since March 23. The district has not corrected or walked back the SPTA list. It has not issued supplemental guidance on how cross-building roles are categorized relative to building-based MTSS positions.

The absence of clarification is itself a signal: if her role were clearly safe, someone would likely have said so by now. The ambiguity is not neutral — it is quietly compressing into a likely decision. Kira should arrive at this meeting prepared for the possibility that the traveling specialist question gets answered for the first time on the record, and that the answer may not be favorable.

---

## Open Questions

- **Is my position on that list?** The SPTA reduction list confirmed MTSS roles are being cut, but nobody has specified whether itinerant and cross-building specialists are bundled into that category or treated separately. I need to know which side of that line I fall on.
- **When they say "MTSS," do they mean the interventionists at each school, or do they mean the whole support layer — including the people who travel between buildings to provide consistency?** Because those are not the same cut, and they don't have the same consequences.
- **If they eliminate or reduce traveling specialist positions, what is the plan for the students at the smaller, lower-enrollment schools who depend on that model for access?** This isn't a rhetorical question — those kids lose services if the model breaks.
- **Has anyone looked at what happens to MTSS wait lists at each building if the support staff is reduced? Do they have that data, or are they cutting without knowing the baseline?**
- **Is the board treating reconfiguration and budget cuts as two separate tracks, or have they connected them yet?** Because the case for redistricting only gets stronger when you lay it next to the staffing reduction list — fewer buildings means fewer travel inefficiencies and more equitable access. If they're not connecting those dots, someone needs to.
- **How are they counting the instructional time lost to inter-building travel in their staffing model?** If that cost isn't visible in the budget documents, it won't factor into the decision.

---

## Agenda Implications

The agenda for the March 30 meeting has not yet been published. Based on Kira's cumulative position and the open threads she is carrying, here is what she should be prepared for:

**Most likely territory:** The board is almost certainly continuing to work through budget reduction scenarios. Given that the SPTA list has been circulating and MTSS roles are confirmed on it, March 30 may be the meeting where those reductions move toward a formal vote or a narrowed set of options. Kira should be prepared for that possibility — not just as an observer, but as someone with professional standing to speak to operational consequences.

**What this means for Kira specifically:**
Her situation is unusual and important: she holds professional knowledge about how the cuts would actually land on students across multiple schools, and she has a personal stake as a parent. That dual lens is a credibility asset at a public comment podium, but it requires her to be precise — if she conflates her job security with her concern for students, she loses the argument. She should lead with the student impact data she has access to, and let the professional jeopardy be subtext.

**What she should listen for:**
- Any characterization of "MTSS reductions" that lumps traveling specialists in with building-based interventionists without distinguishing between them — that framing erases the cross-building equity dimension entirely
- Any board member or administrator framing the cuts as "protected from academic impact" — she has direct knowledge of wait lists and service gaps that contradict that framing
- Whether the reconfiguration and redistricting conversation surfaces at all in connection with the staffing reductions, or whether those are still being siloed
- Any reference to "efficiencies" or "consolidation" in staffing — this language may be used to justify eliminating traveling specialists without naming it directly

**Questions she should prepare:**
- *"The SPTA list shows MTSS reductions. Can administration clarify whether cross-building and itinerant specialist roles are categorized within that reduction or outside it, and how many positions in each category are affected?"*
- *"For the buildings that currently depend on itinerant support because their enrollment doesn't support a full-time specialist — what is the service delivery plan for those students if this model is eliminated or reduced?"*
- *"Has the board or administration analyzed the relationship between the staffing reduction list and the reconfiguration proposals? Specifically, whether redistricting would reduce the structural inefficiencies that make itinerant staffing necessary in the first place?"*

---

## Watch For

- **Any administrative presentation that uses the phrase "MTSS" or "intervention support" without defining the scope** — push for specificity on whether itinerant/traveling roles are included or excluded. Vague language here is not an accident.
- **Watch the superintendent and curriculum/instruction director specifically** when MTSS reductions are discussed. Their framing will signal whether traveling specialist positions are being treated as a distinct category or folded into a broader cut.
- **If any board member asks about equity impacts of the staffing reductions**, that is Kira's opening — she has the operational data to answer that question in a way no single-school parent can.
- **Watch for the moment when the reconfiguration and budget conversations either connect or deliberately don't** — if administration is presenting them as unrelated, that is a position she can challenge with the efficiency data she holds.
- **Listen for any reference to a timeline on the traveling specialist question** — if it's being deferred again, she needs to know that explicitly so she can escalate her advocacy before positions are formally eliminated.
- **If public comment is opened**, watch for whether single-school parents dominate the testimony. Kira's value is in explicitly naming the cross-building perspective — she should be prepared to contrast her view ("I work in three buildings; here is what I see") against testimony that only reflects one school's experience.
- **Monitor whether any board member cites prior committee recommendations** (Boundaries and Configurations) in connection with tonight's decisions — this is an opening to reinforce that the district's own analysis supports redistricting as the structurally sound path.
- **Track body language from board members when equity language comes up** — if eyes go down or there's visible discomfort when MTSS wait list disparities are mentioned, that discomfort is a signal that the data hasn't been surfaced clearly enough yet.
